top_findings.finding: Participant was unable to locate information about intermittent vomiting through multiple navigation strategies and the search function returned no results.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant browsed the 'Browse by Health Topic' section, scrolled through the A–Z index to sections V and B, found no matching entries, then attempted a text search which yielded no results.
top_findings.stated_signal: I'm looking for some health information on intermittent vomiting. Well, it's nothing under that. Let's go to A to Z of Health and see if something's on there. So we're not gonna find anything under those, so let's try typing it in. Mm, nothing has come up with that.
top_findings.rationale: The participant exhausted multiple pathways (topic browse, A–Z alphabetic index, and search) without locating the needed content. Both observed behaviour and stated remarks confirm the required information could not be found within the product, meeting the S2 gate (overall outcome achieved but one component task completely blocked) and L4 calibrator (severe friction requiring external help or task abandonment).

top_findings.finding: Participant encountered a list of known accessibility defects including missing accessible names, text alternatives, and content reflow issues, indicating unresolved accessibility barriers.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant read aloud a detailed list of known issues: non-accessible interactive elements, missing accessible names, inappropriate text, resizing causing loss of content and function, and non-accessible content feedback.
top_findings.stated_signal: Some interactive elements may not be fully accessible to users relying on keyboard and assistive technologies, missing accessible names, inappropriate and missing text, resizing causes loss of content and function.
top_findings.rationale: The participant discovered a formal disclosure of significant accessibility defects affecting keyboard navigation, assistive technology support, and content reflow. This represents a severe accessibility friction event: multiple critical barriers are documented as unresolved, preventing full independent access for users with assistive-technology reliance. The participant did not abandon but acknowledged the barrier.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Keyboard users and those relying on screen readers experience structural amplification through missing accessible names and interactive element barriers. Users requiring zoom or text resizing face additional loss of functionality.

top_findings.finding: Participant expressed confusion about the meaning and cost implications of various health check options and decided to seek help from a person rather than proceed independently.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant read through the add-on options (coronary health check, targeted cancer screening, breast health check) and then stated uncertainty about proceeding; participant decided to chat to somebody instead of continuing with the self-service flow.
top_findings.stated_signal: It doesn't say. You can book some appointments and spread the cost. I think it'd be easier to chat to somebody because it seems a little bit confusing to me. I'm not really sure what any of the other checks we offer means considering there might be money, etc.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated confusion about the meaning of the health check options and their cost implications, leading to the decision to abandon the self-service pathway and seek human assistance. Both observed behaviour (deciding to chat to somebody) and stated confusion indicate a comprehension barrier that forced a workaround, matching S3 (significant component failure worked around) and L3 (significant friction with frustration).

top_findings.finding: Participant could not access detailed content about healthcare offerings on the Bupa website and felt unable to proceed into deeper pages.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant stated they could not go any further into the page to see what healthcare content was available.
top_findings.stated_signal: I can't go any further into the page and see what it is.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated inability to navigate deeper into the page to find healthcare-specific information they were seeking. This represents a content access barrier that prevented them from evaluating the healthcare offering, a significant component of their comparison task. The participant worked around this by comparing only the first pages of the three insurers.

top_findings.finding: Participant could not find the specific health information needed and expressed frustration after reviewing multiple results that did not match their search intent.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant read through content about GERD treatment, then stated dissatisfaction ('I don't think that's really what I'm looking for') and went back. Later, after more content appeared, participant again stated 'I'm not really finding anything that I'm after there' and trailed off.
top_findings.stated_signal: I don't think that's really what I'm looking for, so we'll go back. I'm not really finding anything that I'm after there.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated twice that the search results or content did not match their information need and took corrective action (going back). The observed behaviour of reviewing and rejecting multiple content passages, combined with stated dissatisfaction, indicates a significant content-finding barrier. The participant did not abandon the task but showed clear frustration after multiple failed attempts to locate the desired information.

coaching_recommendations.title: Prioritise task-blocking friction before general coaching
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session contains 78 finding-level issue(s), including task-blocking or near-blocking severity (S2).
advice: Review the highest-severity findings before applying general coaching templates.
Prioritise issues that prevented or substantially disrupted task completion.
Use the highest-severity findings to guide focused follow-up coaching.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A severity distribution: S2=1, S3=3, S4=11, S5=61, S6=2. Representative findings: Participant was unable to locate information about intermittent vomiting through multiple navigation strategies and the search function returned no results. | Participant encountered a list of known accessibility defects including missing accessible names, text alternatives, and content reflow issues, indicating unresolved accessibility barriers. | Participant expressed confusion about the meaning and cost implications of various health check options and decided to seek help from a person rather than proceed independently.

coaching_recommendations.title: Multi-pattern friction across 7 categories — anchor coaching on the dominant pattern
coaching_recommendations.summary: This session shows 78 findings spanning 7 distinct friction types (F1,F2,F6,F5,F3,F7,F4). Coaching should follow the dominant pattern first rather than issuing one item per type.
advice: After addressing any severity-priority recommendation first, use this aggregation to group the remaining coaching themes — do not treat the two as parallel.
Treat F1 (Comprehension) as the primary coaching anchor; secondary types (F2 Confidence, F6 Not Found) provide supporting context.
Avoid issuing parallel recommendations for each friction type when patterns interact within the same session.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A: total=78 findings across 7 distinct friction types (F1=33, F2=19, F6=12, F5=5, F3=4, F7=3, F4=2).
